title: Per capita electricity generation from fossil fuels
caption: Per capita electricity generation from fossil fuels in 2025 varies widely across the globe, ranging from near zero in many regions to over 10,000 kilowatt-hours per person in the highest-consuming nations. The map shows the darkest orange shading—indicating generation above 10,000 kWh per person—concentrated in a small number of countries including Saudi Arabia, the United States, Canada, and Australia. Large parts of Europe, Russia, and East Asia fall into the 2,000 to 5,000 kWh band, shown in medium-dark orange. Much of South America, India, and Southeast Asia appear in lighter shades corresponding to 500 to 2,000 kWh per person. The lowest visible generation levels—below 500 kWh per person—are found across most of sub-Saharan Africa and parts of South Asia. Several countries, particularly in central Africa and Greenland, are marked with diagonal hatching to indicate no available data. The geographic distribution shows high fossil-fuel electricity use per person clustered in wealthy, industrialized, or resource-rich nations, while lower use aligns with less industrialized economies and regions with limited grid access.
